docs.title: Oral History Workflow Guide
docs.text:
A practical guide for oral history researchers at the University of Zurich

Navigate legal requirements, ethical frameworks, and best practices for planning, conducting, and archiving oral history interviews — with tools and templates tailored to the Swiss research context.

Start here 📋 Project Assessment

Answer a few questions about your project and receive a tailored report — including your sensitivity tier, applicable laws, required documents, and an action checklist.

Begin assessment → 🛠 Tools & Equipment

Browse recommended recording equipment, transcription tools, encryption software, and storage solutions — each rated by sensitivity tier suitability.

Explore tools → Quick access Legal & Ethical Framework Sensitivity Tiers (1–5) Consent Form Templates Project Planning Conducting Interviews Processing & Documentation Storage & Security Archiving & Access Glossary & References About this guide. Developed with grant funding for UZH researchers. Content reflects Swiss federal law (nFADP), Zurich cantonal law (IDG), and international oral history standards (OHA). This guide does not constitute legal advice — for project-specific questions, contact the UZH Delegate for Data Protection at datenschutz@dsd.uzh.ch.

Interactive Project Assessment

docs.text:

Step 1 of 7

Project Basics Describe the basic shape of your oral history project. This helps determine which legal and ethical frameworks apply. What recording formats will you use? Select all that apply. Audio only Video Zoom Transcript only Approximately how many narrators do you plan to interview? 1–5 narratorsSmall-scale or pilot project 6–25 narratorsStandard research project 26+ narratorsLarge-scale collection — a Data Protection Impact Assessment may be required Is your project funded by an external grant? docs.title: Step 2 — Sensitivity
docs.text:
1 Basics 2 Sensitivity 3 Risk 4 Jurisdiction 5 Ethics 6 Processing 7 Archiving

Step 2 of 7

Sensitivity Pre-Assessment The topics discussed in your interviews determine the sensitivity tier, which drives consent requirements, storage security, and access restrictions. Think about what narrators are likely to discuss — not just what you plan to ask. Will narrators likely discuss any of the following sensitive categories? Select all that apply. These are "particularly sensitive personal data" under Swiss law (nFADP Art. 5(c), IDG). Political opinions or activitiesParty membership, activism, voting, political persecution Religious or philosophical beliefsFaith, conversion, spiritual practices, worldview Health dataIllness, disability, mental health, medical treatment, substance use Ethnicity or raceEthnic identity, migration experience, discrimination Criminal or administrative proceedingsArrest, trial, legal disputes, sanctions Social assistance or trade unionWelfare, disability benefits, labor organizing Sexual orientation, intimate lifeRelationships, identity, experiences None of the aboveTopics are non-sensitive (e.g., public figure on published work, professional methodology) What level of narrator identification do you anticipate? Narrators will be namedFull name attribution in publications and archives PseudonymsNarrators identified by pseudonyms; key stored separately Fully anonymizedNo possibility of re-identification Mixed — narrator choosesSome named, some pseudonymized ← Back Continue →

docs.title: Step 3 — Risk
docs.text:
1 Basics 2 Sensitivity 3 Risk 4 Jurisdiction 5 Ethics 6 Processing 7 Archiving

Step 3 of 7

Risk Assessment These questions help determine whether your project involves elevated risks that push the sensitivity tier upward and may trigger additional requirements (ethics review, access restrictions, embargoes). Could the content of the interviews cause harm if disclosed? No foreseeable harmContent is non-controversial; narrators are comfortable with disclosure Possible reputational harmNarrators or third parties could face embarrassment, professional consequences Possible serious harmLegal jeopardy, safety risks, re-traumatization, endangerment Does your project involve any of these vulnerable groups? Select all that apply. Minors (under 18) Persons with mental illness or dementia Persons in a dependency relationship with the researcherEmployer–employee, teacher–student, etc. Persons who could be endangeredWhistleblowers, undocumented migrants, persons in conflict zones None of the above Will narrators discuss third parties (other people) in identifiable ways? Unlikely or minimal Likely — names and personal details of others will come up Likely, and the third-party data will be sensitivee.g., discussing someone's health, politics, legal matters ← Back Continue →

docs.title: Step 4 — Jurisdiction
docs.text:
1 Basics 2 Sensitivity 3 Risk 4 Jurisdiction 5 Ethics 6 Processing 7 Archiving

Step 4 of 7

Legal Jurisdiction As a UZH researcher, the Zurich cantonal IDG is your primary data protection law. Additional frameworks may apply depending on your project's scope and collaborations. Does your research focus on human diseases or the structure/function of the human body? If yes, the Human Research Act (HRA/HFG) likely applies, requiring KEK approval. Yese.g., patient experiences, healthcare workers on clinical practice, medical history Possibly — health topics may arise but it is not the focus No Will you interview narrators located outside Switzerland? No — all narrators are in Switzerland Yes — some narrators are in the EU/EEAGDPR may apply in addition to Swiss law Yes — some narrators are outside EU/EEACross-border transfer rules apply; verify adequacy status Will you interview narrators located outside Switzerland? No — UZH only Yes — with other Swiss institutionsData Sharing Agreement needed Yes — international collaborationData Sharing Agreement + cross-border transfer provisions needed ← Back Continue →

docs.title: Step 5 — Ethics
docs.text:
1 Basics 2 Sensitivity 3 Risk 4 Jurisdiction 5 Ethics 6 Processing 7 Archiving

Step 5 of 7

Ethics Review Even when the HRA does not apply, UZH's Ethics Policy recommends ethical review for projects involving certain risk factors. Your faculty's ethics committee can provide guidance. Could participation psychologically harm the narrator? e.g., re-traumatization from discussing distressing experiences, emotional distress. No — topics are not distressing Possible — some topics may be emotionally difficult Likely — the project explicitly addresses traumatic experiences Does your funder or target journal require evidence of ethical review? Yes No / Unsure ← Back Continue →

docs.title: Step 6 — Processing
docs.text:
1 Basics 2 Sensitivity 3 Risk 4 Jurisdiction 5 Ethics 6 Processing 7 Archiving

Step 6 of 7

Data Processing & Tools Using external services to process oral history data triggers UZH's third-party data processing requirements. This includes transcription services, cloud storage, and collaboration tools. How do you plan to transcribe your recordings? Local AI tool (e.g., Whisper on my machine)Data stays on your device — no third-party processing Manual transcription (by myself or research team)No external processing Cloud-based AI service (e.g., Trint, Amberscript, Otter.ai)Data processed on external servers — DPA and GTC required External human transcriber or serviceThird-party processing — GTC + Confidentiality Declaration required Not yet decided Where will you store your recordings and transcripts during the project? UZH infrastructure onlyUZH Research Storage, ScienceCloud, or UZH OneDrive Swiss cloud serviceSWITCHdrive or similar Swiss-hosted service EU cloud servicee.g., institutional Nextcloud, EU-hosted OneDrive Personal devices (laptop, external drive)Encryption required Not yet decided ← Back Continue →

docs.title: Step 7 — Archiving
docs.text:
1 Basics 2 Sensitivity 3 Risk 4 Jurisdiction 5 Ethics 6 Processing 7 Archiving

Step 7 of 7

Archiving & Access Planning for long-term archiving from the start ensures your project meets funder requirements and preserves the oral histories for future use. Do you plan to deposit your oral histories in an archive? Yes — I have identified an archive Yes — but I haven't chosen one yet No / Unsure What access level do you anticipate for the archived materials? This should align with the sensitivity tier. You can always restrict more, but opening access later requires re-consent. Open accessAnyone can access the recordings and transcripts Open with conditionsAttribution required, some excerpts excluded, no commercial use Restricted — registered researchers only Closed / embargoed for a period Not yet decided ← Back Generate Assessment →

docs.title: Archiving & Access
docs.text:
Long-term archiving ensures that your oral histories remain accessible, understandable, and usable beyond the life of your research project. This section covers choosing an archive, preparing materials for deposit, and defining access conditions by tier.

SNSF requirement: Research data must be preserved for at least 10 years and made available according to FAIR principles — "as open as possible, as closed as necessary." Plan your archiving strategy from the start, not at the end of the project.

docs.title: Choosing an Archive
docs.text:
Select an archive based on your sensitivity tier, funder requirements, subject domain, and institutional affiliation. Key criteria:

  • Access control: Can the archive enforce your tier's access restrictions? (Application-based access, embargoes, named-individual access)
  • Format support: Does the archive accept audio, video, and transcript formats?
  • FAIR compliance: Does the archive provide persistent identifiers (DOIs), machine-readable metadata, and standard APIs?
  • Funder acceptance: Does your funder recognize this archive? (SNSF requires FAIR-compliant, non-profit repositories)
  • Geographic location: For Tier 4–5, Swiss-hosted repositories are preferred.

docs.title: International repositories
docs.text:
• re3data.org — Registry of research data repositories. Search by discipline, data type, and access restrictions.
  • OHDA (Oral History Digital Archive) — Specialized oral history platform.

docs.title: Decision guide by tier
docs.text: Tier Archive type Key requirement 1 Any FAIR-compliant repository (Zenodo, SWISSUbase) Public access, DOI 2 Repository with license options (SWISSUbase, Zenodo) CC license with conditions 3 Repository with application-based access (SWISSUbase, DaSCH) Researcher registration, access request 4 Repository with embargo support, or institutional archive Sealed access, time-limited 5 Institutional archive with named-access, or physical storage Named individuals only; consider non-deposit

docs.title: Archival file formats
docs.text:
Prepare two versions: a preservation copy (highest quality, open format) and an access copy (smaller, widely playable):

Content type Preservation format Access format Audio WAV (48 kHz / 24-bit) MP3 (320 kbps) or FLAC Video MOV (ProRes) or MKV (FFV1) MP4 (H.264/H.265) Transcript PDF/A (archival PDF) DOCX or TXT Metadata XML or CSV Same Consent forms PDF/A (scanned) Same

docs.title: Packaging
docs.text:
Organize deposit materials per interview:

deposit/
├── N01/
│   ├── OHWC_N01_20260315_audio_preservation.wav
│   ├── OHWC_N01_20260315_audio_access.mp3
│   ├── OHWC_N01_20260315_transcript_final.pdf
│   ├── OHWC_N01_20260315_metadata.csv
│   └── OHWC_N01_20260315_consent.pdf
├── N02/
│   └── ...
└── project_metadata.csv

Include a README file describing the collection, naming conventions, tier assignments, and contact information.

docs.title: Access Conditions by Tier
docs.text:
Define access conditions in the consent form and communicate them to the archive at deposit.

Tier Access model License Notes 1 — Open Anyone, no application CC BY 4.0 (or CC BY-SA) Full open access. DOI for citation. 2 — Conditional Anyone, with conditions CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Attribution, non-commercial, share-alike. Specific excerpts excluded per consent form. 3 — Restricted Registered researchers, application required Custom access agreement Researcher states purpose. Archive reviews and grants/denies. Typical for sensitive humanities data. 4 — Embargoed Research team only during embargo No public license during embargo Embargo period (10/25/50 years) specified in consent. Post-embargo: review, potentially reclassify to Tier 3 or 2. 5 — Highly restricted Named individuals only No license May not be deposited at all. If deposited, access is by named approval only. Possible scheduled destruction.

docs.title: Data availability statements
docs.text:
When publishing research based on oral history interviews, include a data availability statement. Adapt by tier:

Tier 1–2Tier 3Tier 4–5

"The oral history recordings and transcripts underlying this research are publicly available at [repository name] under [DOI/URL]. Access conditions: [CC license]."

"The oral history data underlying this research are available at [repository name] under restricted access conditions. Qualified researchers may apply for access at [URL]. Access is granted for legitimate academic research purposes."

"The oral history data underlying this research are subject to access restrictions to protect the privacy and safety of participants. Data are archived at [repository/institution] and are not publicly available. Requests for access should be directed to [contact]."

docs.title: Citation practice
docs.text:
Oral history interviews should be cited as primary sources. Recommended format:

[Narrator name or pseudonym], interview by [Interviewer name], [Date], [Project name], [Archive], [Identifier/DOI].

docs.title: Format migration
docs.text:
Digital formats become obsolete. Plan for migration:

  • Store in open, standard formats (WAV, PDF/A, CSV) — not proprietary formats
  • Choose archives with active preservation programs that migrate formats over time
  • SNSF requires minimum 10-year retention

docs.title: Metadata standards for preservation
docs.text:
• Dublin Core — minimum descriptive metadata (SNSF baseline)
  • ISAD(G) — archival description standard (for institutional archives)
  • OHMS (Oral History Metadata Synchronizer) — oral-history-specific metadata with timestamped indexing

docs.title: Review and re-assessment
docs.text:
Plan periodic reviews:

When Action End of project Final quality check, deposit in archive, update DMP Embargo expiry Review access conditions, contact narrator (or representative), potentially reclassify 10-year mark Format check, consider migration, verify continued relevance of access restrictions On request Respond to narrator's exercise of rights (access, correction, deletion)

docs.title: Conducting Interviews
docs.text: The interview is the heart of oral history. This section covers practical and ethical guidance for before, during, and immediately after recording — including handling sensitive disclosures and the recording lead-in protocol.

docs.title: Pre-interview meeting
docs.text:
A pre-interview meeting (in person, by phone, or by video) is strongly recommended. This is not the interview itself — it is a conversation that:

  • Builds rapport and trust between you and the narrator
  • Explains the project, process, and what to expect on recording day
  • Allows the narrator to ask questions about consent, access, and their rights
  • Gives you a sense of the narrator's communication style and likely topics
  • Helps you refine your interview guide for this specific narrator
  • Provides the Information Sheet (Stage 1 of the two-stage consent process)

This meeting is especially important for Tiers 3–5, where sensitive topics may arise.

docs.title: Interview guide
docs.text:
Prepare a guide — not a script. An oral history interview should be a conversation directed by the narrator's memories and perspectives, not a questionnaire. Your guide should contain:

  • Opening questions — broad, inviting: "Tell me about…" "What was it like when…"
  • Topic areas — key themes you want to explore, but as prompts, not fixed questions
  • Follow-up probes — "Can you tell me more about that?" "What happened next?" "How did that make you feel?"
  • Closing questions — "Is there anything we haven't discussed that you'd like to add?" "Is there anything you'd like to go back to?"

The Smithsonian principle

The Smithsonian Folklife Interview Guide emphasizes that the best oral history interviews feel like conversations, not interrogations. Prepare thoroughly, then be ready to set your guide aside and follow the narrator's lead.

docs.title: Technical setup
docs.text:
Always run a test before the interview (Smithsonian Archives recommendation). Arrive early to:

  • Set up and position recording equipment
  • Check audio levels — have the narrator speak at normal volume and verify levels
  • Ensure adequate lighting (for video)
  • Minimize background noise (close windows, turn off fans/AC if possible)
  • Verify storage space on recording device
  • Have backup batteries and SD cards ready
  • For remote interviews: test connection, verify local recording is enabled, test backup recording

docs.title: Recording lead-in
docs.text:
Begin every recording with a spoken lead-in that identifies the session. This becomes critical metadata. Adapt by tier:

Tier 1–2 (Open)Tier 3 (Restricted)Tier 4–5 (Closed)

"This is [your name], interviewing [narrator's full name] on [date] at [location] for the [project name] at the University of Zurich. This is session [number]."

"This is [your name], interviewing [narrator's pseudonym or ID code] on [date] at [general location, e.g., 'Zurich'] for the [project name] at the University of Zurich. This is session [number]."

Do not state the narrator's real name on the recording if they will be pseudonymized.

"This is [your name], recording session [narrator ID code] on [date] for [project code] at UZH. Session [number]."

Use codes only. No names, no specific locations. Identifying information is recorded on paper, stored separately from the recording.

docs.title: Best practices during the interview
docs.text:
Listen actively. The most important skill in oral history is listening. Resist the urge to fill silences — pauses often precede the most thoughtful responses.

Follow the narrator's lead. If the conversation moves away from your guide into unexpected territory, follow it. Some of the most valuable oral history content emerges from unplanned directions.

Ask open-ended questions. "Tell me about…" not "Did you like…?" "What was that experience like?" not "Was it difficult?"

Respect refusal. If the narrator declines to answer a question or signals discomfort, accept this immediately and move on. You may note the topic for discussion after the recording.

Avoid leading questions. Don't suggest the answer you expect. "What happened next?" not "That must have been terrible, right?"

Take brief notes. Jot down follow-up points, timestamps for key moments, and technical notes (noise interruptions, etc.) without breaking the flow.

docs.title: Handling sensitive disclosures
docs.text:
During an interview, a narrator may unexpectedly disclose something highly sensitive — a traumatic experience, a criminal matter, information about a third party, or something that could put them at risk.

If the narrator becomes distressed:

  1. Pause the recording if appropriate, or ask: "Would you like me to stop recording for a moment?"
  2. Acknowledge the difficulty: "Thank you for sharing that. I can see this is a difficult topic."
  3. Remind them they are free to stop at any time or to exclude this portion from the record
  4. Do not press for more detail
  5. After the interview, discuss whether this passage should be excluded, embargoed, or access-restricted

If the disclosure changes the sensitivity tier:

If what the narrator reveals pushes the interview from your planned tier into a higher one (e.g., from Tier 2 to Tier 4), note this for the post-interview consent discussion. You may need to use a different consent form than originally planned.

If a third party is discussed in a damaging or sensitive way:

Note the passage for possible anonymization. Discuss with the narrator during the consent stage whether and how to handle it.

Do not promise confidentiality you cannot guarantee

Be honest with narrators about what you can and cannot protect. If a recording exists, there is always some risk — however small — of unauthorized access. The tier system mitigates this risk but cannot eliminate it entirely.

docs.title: Immediate steps (within 48 hours)
docs.text:
1. Back up the recording immediately. Apply the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 different media types, 1 off-site. Copy from the recorder's SD card to UZH Research Storage and to an encrypted USB drive.
  2. Write field notes while the interview is fresh. Note key topics, timestamps for significant passages, technical issues, emotional tone, and anything the narrator said off-record that affects interpretation.
  3. Begin metadata entry. Record: narrator ID, date, location, duration, format, interviewer, language, topics covered, sensitivity notes.

docs.title: Finalizing consent (Stage 2)
docs.text:
After the interview — ideally on the same day or within a week — review the consent form together with the narrator:

  • Walk through the recording and identify any passages to exclude
  • Confirm the access level (tier) and identification preference (named / pseudonym)
  • For Tier 4: agree on the embargo period
  • Sign the consent form
  • Provide the narrator with a copy

docs.title: Post-interview debrief
docs.text: For interviews involving traumatic or emotionally difficult content, plan a debrief — both for the narrator and for yourself. Working with difficult testimony can affect researchers. UZH offers counseling services for staff; consider using them if needed.

docs.title: Remote Interview Checklist
docs.text:
For interviews conducted via Zoom, Riverside, or other remote tools:

  • Verify the recording tool's data processing location and DPA status (see Legal Framework §2.8)
  • Enable local recording (not cloud) in Zoom
  • Ask the narrator to use a quiet, private room
  • Test audio quality with the narrator before starting
  • Use a backup: run OBS locally to capture the call as a secondary recording
  • Send the consent form electronically before the interview; collect a signed copy after
  • For Tiers 4–5: do not use cloud-based tools. Each participant records locally; files are transferred via SWITCHfilesender or encrypted email

docs.title: Project Planning & Design
docs.text:
Good planning is the foundation of ethical, legally compliant oral history research. Decisions made at the design stage — about narrators, topics, formats, and data management — determine everything that follows. This section walks you through the essential planning steps.

Before you begin: Complete the interactive project assessment to determine your sensitivity tier and applicable legal requirements. The results will inform every planning decision below.

docs.title: Define your research questions
docs.text:
Oral history is uniquely suited to capturing subjective experience, memory, and personal perspective. Before designing interviews, clarify what your project aims to understand and why oral history is the appropriate method. This framing shapes everything: who you interview, what you ask, and how you handle the resulting data.

Consider: What gap in the historical or scholarly record does this project address? Whose voices are missing? What can oral testimony reveal that written sources cannot?

docs.title: Identify and approach narrators
docs.text:
The OHA Principles & Best Practices (2018) emphasize the importance of seeking diverse perspectives, including voices that have been historically underrepresented. When identifying potential narrators:

  • Consider whose perspectives are essential to the research questions
  • Seek diversity of experience within your topic
  • Be attentive to power dynamics between researcher and narrator
  • For community-based projects, engage with community representatives early
  • Respect that some individuals may decline — participation must always be voluntary

First contact should explain the project clearly and without pressure. Use an institutional email (UZH) or, for narrators who prefer privacy, encrypted communication (ProtonMail, Threema). Provide the Information Sheet (see Consent Templates) at this stage.

docs.title: Conduct an early sensitivity pre-assessment
docs.text:
Before any interviews take place, estimate the likely sensitivity tier for your project. Think about what narrators are likely to discuss — not just what you plan to ask. Oral history conversations often go in unexpected directions.

Ask yourself:

  • Will narrators discuss health, politics, religion, ethnicity, or legal matters?
  • Could disclosure cause harm to narrators or people they mention?
  • Are any narrators in vulnerable groups?
  • Will identifiable third parties come up?

Use the interactive assessment tool for a structured evaluation, or consult the Sensitivity Classification Model.

docs.title: Plan your budget
docs.text:
Oral history projects require budgeting for:

Category Items to consider Equipment Audio recorder, microphones, camera, tripod, lighting, cables, SD cards, batteries Transcription Software licenses (f4transkript), AI tools, or external transcription services Travel Travel to narrator locations, accommodation if needed Storage Encrypted USB drives, VeraCrypt licenses (free), additional UZH storage if needed Archiving Repository deposit fees (if applicable), format migration Personnel Research assistants, transcribers, technical support Compensation Narrator compensation or honoraria (if applicable — check ethics implications) Dissemination Publication costs, website hosting, event costs

docs.title: Write your Data Management Plan (DMP)
docs.text:
A DMP is required by most funders and is good practice regardless. It forces you to think through the entire data lifecycle — from collection through archiving.

SNSFERC / EUNo external funding

The SNSF requires a DMP submitted via mySNF. For oral history, address:

  • Data collection: Audio/video recordings, transcripts, metadata, consent forms
  • Documentation and metadata: Dublin Core minimum; oral-history-specific fields (interviewer, narrator, date, location, topics, language, duration)
  • Ethics and legal compliance: Reference the IDG, your sensitivity tier, and consent process
  • Storage and security: UZH infrastructure, encryption, backup strategy (3-2-1 rule)
  • Data sharing and reuse: FAIR principles — "as open as possible, as closed as necessary"
  • Archiving: Identify the repository (see Archiving & Access), file formats, retention period

ERC requires a DMP following the FAIR data management structure. Emphasis on:

  • FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable)
  • Open access mandate — explain any restrictions clearly
  • GDPR compliance if EU narrators or collaborators are involved
  • Data availability statement for publications

Even without a funder requirement, writing a DMP is strongly recommended. Use the SNSF template as a guide — it covers all essential elements. This protects you, your narrators, and the long-term value of the data.

docs.title: Choose your recording format
docs.text:
Format Best for Considerations Audio only Most projects; lower complexity; narrator may be more comfortable without a camera Archival format: WAV (48 kHz / 24-bit minimum). Easier to anonymize (no visual identification). Video Projects where visual context matters (gestures, objects, location); institutional or community archives Higher storage needs. Harder to anonymize (face blurring needed). Requires additional equipment (camera, lighting, tripod). Remote (Zoom / Riverside) Geographically dispersed narrators; pandemic or mobility constraints Data protection implications for cloud tools. Quality lower than in-person. Use local recording, not cloud. Transcript only When recording is not possible or narrator refuses recording Loses vocal nuance, pauses, emotion. Not ideal for archival oral history. Use as supplement, not replacement.

See Tools & Equipment for specific recommendations by tier.

docs.title: Prepare consent materials
docs.text:
Based on your sensitivity tier, prepare:

  • Information Sheet (all tiers) — provided before the interview
  • Consent Form (tier-specific) — signed after the interview
  • Confidentiality Declaration (if using external transcribers or assistants)

See Consent Form Templates for ready-to-adapt templates based on the UZH Declaration of Consent.

docs.title: Set up file management
docs.text:
Establish your file naming convention and folder structure before the first interview. Consistent naming from the start prevents chaos later.

Recommended naming convention (adapted from SNSF example):

[ProjectAbbreviation]_[NarratorID]_[Date]_[Type].[ext]

Examples:
OHWC_N01_20260315_audio.wav
OHWC_N01_20260315_transcript_v1.docx
OHWC_N01_20260315_consent.pdf
OHWC_N01_20260315_metadata.csv

Folder structure:

project-root/
├── 01-recordings/          # Original audio/video files
│   ├── N01/
│   └── N02/
├── 02-transcripts/         # Transcripts (versions)
│   ├── N01/
│   └── N02/
├── 03-consent-forms/       # Signed consent forms (scanned)
├── 04-metadata/            # Metadata spreadsheets
├── 05-anonymized/          # Anonymized versions for sharing
├── 06-analysis/            # Working analysis files
└── 07-admin/               # Project management, correspondence

docs.title: Planning Timeline
docs.text:
When Action Months before first interview Define research questions. Write DMP. Complete project assessment. Prepare consent materials. Purchase/test equipment. Set up storage and folder structure. Weeks before Contact narrators. Send Information Sheet. Schedule pre-interview meetings. Days before Test equipment. Prepare interview guide. Confirm logistics. Day of Arrive early. Set up and test. Conduct pre-interview conversation. Record. Sign consent form. Within 48 hours Back up recordings (3-2-1 rule). Write field notes. Begin metadata entry.

The golden rule

Plan for the most sensitive scenario you might encounter, even if you expect most interviews to be straightforward. It is much easier to have processes in place and not need them than to scramble when a narrator unexpectedly discloses something highly sensitive.

docs.title: Processing & Documentation
docs.text: After the interview, the work of transforming raw recordings into structured, documented, and usable research data begins. This section covers file management, metadata, transcription, anonymization, and quality control.

docs.title: Naming convention
docs.text:
Use consistent naming from the first file. Recommended format:

[Project]_[NarratorID]_[YYYYMMDD]_[Type]_[Version].[ext]

OHWC_N01_20260315_audio.wav
OHWC_N01_20260315_transcript_v1.docx
OHWC_N01_20260315_transcript_v2_reviewed.docx
OHWC_N01_20260315_consent.pdf

Rules: no spaces (use underscores or hyphens), no special characters, include the date in ISO format (YYYYMMDD), version transcripts explicitly.

docs.title: Backup: the 3-2-1 rule
docs.text:
The Smithsonian Archives of American Art recommends the 3-2-1 rule as the minimum backup standard:

  • 3 copies of every file
  • 2 different storage media (e.g., UZH Research Storage + encrypted USB)
  • 1 copy off-site (e.g., SWITCHdrive or a second UZH location)

Back up immediately after the interview — do not leave original recordings only on the recorder's SD card.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Good metadata makes your oral histories findable, understandable, and reusable. Create metadata for each interview at the time of processing, not months later.

docs.title: Minimum metadata (Dublin Core)
docs.text:
The SNSF requires Dublin Core as a baseline. For oral history, record at minimum:

Field Description Example Title Descriptive title of the interview "Interview with [Narrator ID] on workplace culture at [Institution]" Creator Interviewer name "Dr. Maria Schneider" Subject Topic keywords "workplace culture; institutional history; 1990s" Description Brief summary of content "Narrator discusses their 20-year career at…" Date Date of recording (ISO) "2026-03-15" Type Resource type "Sound" or "MovingImage" Format File format and technical details "audio/wav; 48kHz/24bit; 87 min" Language Language(s) spoken "de" or "en" Rights Access conditions "Restricted: registered researchers. Tier 3." Identifier Unique project ID "OHWC_N01_20260315"

docs.title: Oral-history-specific metadata
docs.text:
Beyond Dublin Core, add:

Field Description Narrator ID / pseudonym Identifier or pseudonym used Location Where the interview took place (city level for Tier 3+) Duration Total recording length Session number If multiple sessions with the same narrator Sensitivity tier Assigned tier (1–5) Consent status "Signed" / "Pending review" / date Transcription status "Not started" / "AI draft" / "Reviewed" / "Final" Anonymization status "Not required" / "Completed" / "Pending" Excluded passages Timestamps of passages excluded per narrator's request Third-party data Timestamps of passages containing identifiable third-party data

Use a spreadsheet

Maintain a single metadata spreadsheet (Excel or CSV) for the entire project. One row per interview. This becomes the master index and is essential for archiving.

docs.title: Transcription approaches
docs.text:
Approach When to use Effort Quality Full verbatim Primary research output; linguistic or discourse analysis; archival preservation High Highest — captures speech as spoken, including pauses, false starts, filler words Clean verbatim Most oral history projects; readability matters Medium High — light editing for readability while preserving meaning and voice Summary / index Large collections where full transcription is not feasible; preliminary cataloguing Low Lower — captures topics and timestamps, not exact words

For most oral history projects, clean verbatim is recommended: edit for readability (remove excessive filler words, correct obvious false starts) while preserving the narrator's voice, dialect, and speech patterns.

docs.title: Transcription workflow
docs.text:
1. AI first pass — Use local Whisper to generate an initial transcript. This is typically 80–95% accurate depending on audio quality and language.
  2. Human review and correction — Listen to the recording while reading the transcript. Correct errors, add speaker labels, mark unclear passages with [inaudible] or [unclear].
  3. Formatting — Apply consistent formatting: speaker labels (Interviewer: / Narrator:), timestamps at regular intervals, paragraph breaks at topic changes.
  4. Narrator review (if consented) — Provide the transcript to the narrator for review. They may correct factual errors, clarify unclear passages, or request removal of specific sections.
  5. Final version — Incorporate narrator feedback. Mark the version as final. Update metadata.

docs.title: Transcription tools by tier
docs.text:
Tier Recommended approach 1–2 Local Whisper for first pass, or cloud service (Amberscript, Trint) with DPA. Manual correction. 3 Local Whisper preferred. Cloud only with Swiss/EU DPA. Manual correction. 4–5 Local Whisper or fully manual (f4transkript, oTranscribe). No cloud processing.

See Transcription Tools for detailed tool recommendations.

docs.title: When is anonymization required?
docs.text: Situation Required? Third-party data in publications (all tiers) Yes — always anonymize identifiable third parties unless they are public figures in public capacity and have consented Narrator data in publications (Tier 3+) Yes — use pseudonyms unless narrator has explicitly consented to name attribution Narrator data in the archive (Tier 3+) Depends on access model — restricted access may substitute for anonymization of originals Narrator data in the archive (Tier 1–2) No — narrator has consented to identifiable access

docs.title: Anonymization vs. pseudonymization
docs.text:
Under Swiss law these are distinct concepts:

  • Anonymization: All identifying information is irreversibly removed. The data can no longer be linked to any individual. Anonymized data falls outside the scope of the Data Protection Act.
  • Pseudonymization: Identifying information is replaced with codes or pseudonyms, but a key exists that could re-link the data to the individual. Pseudonymized data is still personal data under the law.

For oral history, full anonymization is often impractical (a voice recording is inherently identifiable). The practical approach is pseudonymization of transcripts and outputs, with access restrictions on the original recordings.

docs.title: How to anonymize transcripts
docs.text:
1. Replace names with consistent pseudonyms: [NARRATOR-A], [COLLEAGUE-B], [INSTITUTION-C]. Use a lookup style that reveals the category but not the identity.
  2. Replace identifying details: Specific job titles → "a senior role"; exact dates → "in the early 2000s"; distinctive locations → "a hospital in Zurich."
  3. Maintain a de-identification key — a separate, securely stored document mapping pseudonyms to real identifiers. Store this at the same or higher security level as the original data.
  4. Mark redacted passages clearly in the transcript: [PASSAGE EXCLUDED AT NARRATOR'S REQUEST, 23:14–24:52]

docs.title: How to anonymize audio and video
docs.text:
• Audio: Use Audacity to silence or tone-replace identifying segments (names, locations). Generate a tone (e.g., 400 Hz beep) over the relevant portion.
  • Video: Use DaVinci Resolve to blur faces, obscure identifying backgrounds, and silence audio passages.

Anonymization is harder than it looks

Even with names removed, combinations of details (employer + time period + role + specific events) can re-identify individuals. Consider context-based identification risk, especially for Tier 3+ interviews in small professional communities.

docs.title: Quality Control
docs.text:
Before moving to archiving, verify:

  • Recording quality: Audio is clear, complete, no major technical failures
  • Transcript accuracy: Reviewed against recording, speaker labels correct, timestamps present
  • Metadata completeness: All required fields filled, consistent with naming convention
  • Consent verification: Signed consent form on file, matches the sensitivity tier assigned
  • Anonymization status: Third-party data anonymized in transcripts; de-identification key stored separately
  • Excluded passages: All passages excluded at narrator's request are clearly marked and (if applicable) removed from access copies
  • File naming: Consistent, no spaces or special characters, versioned correctly
  • Backup status: 3-2-1 rule satisfied

docs.title: Storage & Security
docs.text:
Secure storage is a legal obligation under both the Zurich IDG (§ 7) and the nFADP (Art. 8). This section covers where and how to store oral history data during the active research phase — from recordings and transcripts to consent forms and de-identification keys.

Core principle: The level of storage security must match the sensitivity tier. All tiers require baseline measures (encryption, backup). Higher tiers require progressively stricter access controls, encryption layers, and infrastructure choices.

docs.title: Storage by Tier
docs.text:
Tier 1–2Tier 3Tier 4Tier 5

Primary storage: UZH Research Storage, UZH OneDrive/SharePoint, or SWITCHdrive.

Requirements:

  • Full-disk encryption on all devices (BitLocker / FileVault) — mandatory
  • Regular backups (3-2-1 rule)
  • UZH account authentication for access
  • No storage on unencrypted USB drives

Primary storage: UZH Research Storage or UZH ScienceCloud. SWITCHdrive acceptable for collaboration copies.

Additional requirements:

  • Access restricted to named team members (not shared-by-default)
  • VeraCrypt containers for any portable copies (USB, laptop)
  • Consent forms stored separately from recordings
  • De-identification key stored in a separate encrypted location from the anonymized data
  • No personal cloud services (Dropbox, Google Drive, iCloud)

Primary storage: UZH Research Storage or UZH ScienceCloud only.

Additional requirements:

  • VeraCrypt-encrypted containers for all oral history files
  • Named-individual access permissions (not group/team)
  • Consent forms in a locked physical location (secure filing cabinet, restricted office)
  • De-identification key on a separate encrypted device from the data
  • Access log: record who accessed what and when
  • No cloud services for original recordings
  • Physical security for any paper documents

Primary storage: UZH Research Storage with named-individual access only.

Additional requirements:

  • All Tier 4 requirements plus:
  • Consider air-gapped storage for the most sensitive material (dedicated device not connected to any network)
  • Separate VeraCrypt passwords for different components (recordings, transcripts, keys)
  • Physical access controls (locked room, restricted key holders)
  • Consider whether material should be stored on UZH infrastructure at all, or whether a dedicated secure device is more appropriate
  • Plan for destruction if specified in the consent form

docs.title: Full-disk encryption (mandatory for all tiers)
docs.text:
Every device that touches oral history data must have full-disk encryption enabled:

OS Tool How to enable Windows BitLocker Settings → Privacy & Security → Device encryption (or BitLocker in older versions). Requires Windows Pro/Enterprise. macOS FileVault System Settings → Privacy & Security → FileVault → Turn On. Linux LUKS Configure during installation. For existing systems: cryptsetup luksFormat.

docs.title: File-level encryption (Tier 3+)
docs.text:
For additional protection, encrypt individual files or folders using VeraCrypt containers:

  1. Download VeraCrypt (free, open source, cross-platform)
  2. Create a new encrypted volume (container file)
  3. Set a strong password (20+ characters, stored in your password manager)
  4. Mount the container when working, dismount when done
  5. The container appears as a regular file — easy to back up and transport

For cloud storage: Use Cryptomator to encrypt files before they sync to cloud services. This adds a transparent encryption layer — files are encrypted on your device and only encrypted versions reach the cloud.

docs.title: Password management
docs.text:
Use a password manager for all project-related passwords:

Tier Recommended tool 1–3 Bitwarden (cloud-synced, team sharing) or KeePassXC (local) 4–5 KeePassXC (local database, no cloud sync). Store the database on an encrypted device.

docs.title: Secure Data Transfer
docs.text:
When transferring oral history data — to collaborators, archives, or narrators for review — use secure channels:

Method Suitable for Notes SWITCHfilesender Large files to Swiss university recipients Swiss-hosted, temporary links, up to 250 GB. Good for Tiers 1–4. UZH OneDrive shared link Small files within UZH or to external collaborators Set link expiry and password. Suitable for Tiers 1–3. Encrypted email attachment (7-Zip AES-256) Small files to anyone Create a password-protected archive. Send the password via a different channel (e.g., phone, Signal). ProtonMail Sensitive communications, especially with narrators Swiss-hosted, end-to-end encryption. Free tier available. Suitable for all tiers. Physical handoff Tier 5 material, consent forms Encrypted USB drive, handed over in person. Sometimes the safest method.

Never send unencrypted sensitive recordings by email

Standard email is not encrypted end-to-end. Do not attach oral history recordings (Tier 3+) to regular email. Use SWITCHfilesender, encrypted archives, or ProtonMail.

docs.title: Third-Party Processing: UZH Requirements
docs.text: When UZH data is processed by external parties (transcription services, cloud platforms), specific contracts are required. This is governed by § 6 IDG and UZH's standard documents.

docs.title: Required documents by scenario
docs.text:
Scenario Required documents ICT service in Switzerland GTC for ICT Services (CSI) + GTC DP Outsourcing IT UZH + Confidentiality Declaration ICT service in EU/EEA Same as above ICT service in third country (no adequate protection) All above + EU Standard Contractual Clauses (UZH version) Non-ICT service in Switzerland (e.g., human transcriber) GTC DP Processing Third Parties UZH + Confidentiality Declaration Non-ICT service in third country Same + EU Standard Contractual Clauses

Contact the UZH Delegate for Data Protection (datenschutz@dsd.uzh.ch) for the current versions of these documents.

docs.title: DMP in Practice
docs.text:
Your Data Management Plan should describe your actual storage setup. When filling in the SNSF or ERC DMP, map your oral history storage as follows:

DMP question Your answer should cover Where will data be stored during the project? UZH Research Storage / ScienceCloud. Encrypted devices for fieldwork. How will data be backed up? 3-2-1 rule: UZH storage + encrypted USB + SWITCHdrive (or second UZH location). Who will have access? Named team members. Access permissions match the sensitivity tier. What security measures are in place? Full-disk encryption, VeraCrypt containers (Tier 3+), password manager, access controls. How will personal data be protected? Pseudonymization of transcripts, restricted access to originals, separate storage of de-identification keys. Will data be processed outside Switzerland? State whether any tools or services process data outside CH/EU. Reference DPA and GTC.

docs.title: Storage Checklist
docs.text:
• Full-disk encryption enabled on all devices (BitLocker / FileVault)
  • Primary storage on UZH infrastructure (not personal cloud)
  • 3-2-1 backup rule implemented
  • VeraCrypt containers for portable copies (Tier 3+)
  • Consent forms stored separately from recordings
  • De-identification key stored separately from anonymized data
  • Password manager in use for all project credentials
  • Third-party processing contracts executed (if using external services)
  • Access permissions match the sensitivity tier
  • Storage setup documented in the DMP

docs.title: Consent Form Templates
docs.text:
All consent forms below are adapted from the UZH Declaration of Consent for Research Projects (version May 2018), supplemented with oral-history-specific provisions for each sensitivity tier. The language stays as close to the UZH original as possible, deviating only where oral history requires additional clauses.

Two-stage consent: Use the Information Sheet (Stage 1) before the interview. Use the Consent Form (Stage 2) after the interview, when the narrator can make informed decisions about access, pseudonymization, and excluded passages based on what was actually discussed.

docs.title: Information Sheet (Stage 1 — All Tiers)
docs.text:
This is provided to the narrator before the interview. It does not require a signature — it fulfills the duty to inform under Swiss data protection law.

Information Sheet for Participants in an Oral History Research Project

Title of research project:

Lead researcher:

Organizational unit at UZH:

Funded by:

Dear ,

You are invited to participate in an oral history research project conducted at the University of Zurich. This information sheet explains the purpose, methods, and your rights. Please read it carefully. You will have the opportunity to ask questions before and after the interview.

What is the purpose of this project?

(describe the research objectives in plain language)

What will happen?

You will be interviewed by about your experiences, memories, and perspectives on . The interview will be recorded in (audio / video / audio and video) format. The interview will last approximately minutes. After the interview, a transcript will be produced.

What personal data will be collected?

The recording will capture your voice (and image, if video). During the interview, you may share personal experiences, opinions, and memories. Some of this may constitute sensitive personal data under Swiss data protection law (e.g., health experiences, political views, religious beliefs). You are free to decline to answer any question.

How will your data be processed and stored?

Your recording and transcript will be stored on secure University of Zurich infrastructure. Access is restricted to the research team: (list team members). The data will be processed exclusively for academic research purposes.

Who will have access?

(Tier-specific: describe the access model — public / registered researchers / research team only / named individuals)

How long will your data be kept?

As a rule, the data is kept for a ten-year period and then deleted or destroyed, or has identifying information removed, unless permission has been given for its continued use in other research projects.

Your rights

You may at any time:

  • Request information about the data stored about you
  • Demand the correction, deletion, or sealing of your personal data
  • Object to the processing or transmission of your data
  • Withdraw your consent (see Consent Form for details)
  • Lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority
Contact

For questions about this project: (lead researcher email) For data protection concerns: UZH Delegate for Data Protection, datenschutz@dsd.uzh.ch

docs.title: Consent Forms by Tier (Stage 2)
docs.text:
The following consent forms are signed after the interview. Each builds on the UZH template with tier-specific additions.

Tier 1 — Open Consent Form: Oral History Interview (Tier 1) Declaration of Consent for Research Projects

(University of Zurich, adapted for oral history)

By Ms / Mr:

Title of research project:

Organizational unit responsible at UZH:

Lead researcher:

I hereby consent that the following personal data may be recorded from me:

Audio recording ☐   Video recording ☐   Written transcript ☐

in connection with the oral history research project with the following objective: .

An individual within the responsible organizational unit at UZH, (first name and last name), has comprehensively explained the purpose, importance and scope of gathering such data, as well as the precise way and location in which my data will be processed and the research project conducted. In this connection I had the opportunity to ask questions, understood the answers, and currently have no further questions about the research project. I had sufficient time to decide whether or not to participate in this research project.

I am taking part voluntarily. I have been notified that I may revoke my consent at any time for the future, without giving reasons, and that all of the data that has been gathered up to that point will be deleted and destroyed, providing the deletion and destruction of the collected data does not make it impossible to achieve the objectives of the research or seriously jeopardizes their achievement. To revoke my consent, I will contact the aforementioned responsible organizational unit.

Publication and identification My data may be used in a form that allows conclusions to be drawn about my identity (full name attribution). Access conditions I consent to the interview recording and transcript being made publicly accessible without restrictions.

Once the research project has been concluded, the personal data will be handed over to (name of archive) to be archived. Place, date / signature Tier 3 — Restricted Access Consent Form: Oral History Interview (Tier 3) Declaration of Consent for Research Projects

(University of Zurich, adapted for oral history — Restricted Access)

in connection with the oral history research project with the following objective: .
