Place, date / signature

Tiers 2 and 5

Tier 2 uses the same structure as Tier 1 but adds: pseudonym option, excluded-passages clause, and conditions (e.g., no commercial use, attribution required). Tier 5 uses the Tier 4 structure but adds: named-individual access lists, potential destruction clause, and heightened re-consent requirements. Full templates for all five tiers will be available as downloadable .docx files.

docs.title: UZH Confidentiality Declaration
docs.text:
Required for external persons (transcribers, research assistants, collaborators) who will have access to oral history data. Based on the UZH Confidentiality Declaration (Legal Services and Data Protection, January 2025).

Key obligations for signatories:

  • Maintain confidentiality of all non-public information
  • Comply with the Zurich IDG
  • Process data only for the stipulated purposes
  • Return or destroy all data upon termination
  • Report any data loss or unauthorized access immediately

→ Obtain the template from UZH Legal Services / Delegate for Data Protection (datenschutz@dsd.uzh.ch)

docs.title: Data Sharing Agreement
docs.text:
For sharing oral history data with external institutions, use the UZH Master Data Sharing Agreement (Controller–Controller). This covers data categories, purposes, security measures, breach notification, and data subject rights.

→ Obtain the template from UZH Legal Services

docs.title: Legal & Ethical Framework
docs.text:
This section covers the data protection and ethical framework that applies to oral history research at the University of Zurich. Understanding which laws govern your project is the essential first step.

Key point: As a cantonal institution, UZH is primarily governed by the Zurich Act on Information and Data Protection (IDG) — not the federal nFADP. However, the nFADP and potentially the GDPR may also apply depending on your project's scope and collaborations. This section helps you navigate all three layers.

docs.title: 2.1 Which Law Applies?
docs.text:
Swiss data protection law is not a single statute. Which law governs your oral history project depends on your institutional affiliation and the nature of your research.

Primary — applies to all UZH projects Zurich Act on Information and Data Protection (IDG)

As a cantonal institution, UZH is governed by the Zurich Gesetz über die Information und den Datenschutz (IDG) and its implementing ordinance (IDV). All UZH employees are bound by official secrecy (Amtsgeheimnis) under the IDG without needing to sign an additional confidentiality declaration.

Key provisions for oral history:

  • § 6 IDG — Third-party processing: External services (transcription, cloud hosting) require specific legal, technical, and organizational measures. UZH has standard documents for this (see Section 2.8).
  • § 7 IDG — Security measures: Appropriate technical and organizational measures are required to protect information.
  • § 8–9 IDG — Proportionality and purpose limitation: Data may only be processed to the extent necessary and for the stated purpose.

Supervisory authority: Cantonal Data Protection Commissioner (Datenschutzbeauftragte des Kantons Zürich) → datenschutz.ch

Reference standard — always relevant Federal Act on Data Protection (nFADP / revDSG)

The revised Bundesgesetz über den Datenschutz (DSG), entered into force on 1 September 2023. It applies to private persons and federal bodies (including ETH Zurich, but not cantonal universities like UZH).

UZH researchers must still understand it because:

  • It applies when collaborating with private-sector partners or federal institutions
  • It defines the national baseline for concepts like sensitive data, consent, and cross-border transfer
  • Grant funders (SNSF, ERC) may reference federal or EU-level standards
  • It carries personal criminal liability of up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations

Supervisory authority: Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC/EDÖB) → edoeb.admin.ch

Conditional — only if health-related Human Research Act (HRA / HFG)

The Humanforschungsgesetz governs research on human diseases and the structure/function of the human body. Most oral history projects do not fall under the HRA. However, it may apply if:

  • Interviews focus on health experiences (patient oral histories, healthcare workers on clinical practice)
  • The research involves biological material or health data collected specifically for the project

If the HRA applies → mandatory approval by the Cantonal Ethics Commission Zurich (KEK) before data collection.

→ KEK Zurich

Quick decision

Is your research about human diseases or the structure/function of the human body? Yes → contact KEK. No → the HRA does not apply, but faculty-level ethical review and data protection obligations remain.

docs.title: 2.2 nFADP vs. GDPR
docs.text:
If your project involves narrators in the EU, cross-border collaboration, or EU-funded grants, you may also need to comply with the GDPR. Key differences:

Aspect nFADP (Switzerland) GDPR (EU) Sensitive data categories Includes genetic + biometric (since 2023) Same categories Consent for sensitive data Explicit consent required Explicit consent required DPO requirement Not mandatory Mandatory in certain cases Breach notification "As soon as possible" to FDPIC Within 72 hours Fines Up to CHF 250,000 on individuals Up to €20M / 4% turnover on organizations Research exemption Art. 31(2)(e): narrow Art. 89: broader DPIA Required for high-risk processing Required for high-risk processing

Practical takeaway

If you already comply with the GDPR (e.g., for an ERC project), you will meet most nFADP requirements. The main additional Swiss concern is personal criminal liability for intentional violations.

docs.title: 2.3 Sensitive Personal Data in Oral History
docs.text:
Understanding what counts as "sensitive" under Swiss law determines consent requirements, storage security, and access restrictions.

Under both the nFADP (Art. 5(c)) and the Zurich IDG, the following are particularly sensitive (besonders schützenswerte Personendaten):

Category Common in oral history? Examples Religious or philosophical beliefs Yes Faith journey, conversion, spiritual practices Political opinions or activities Yes Political activism, party membership, voting Health data Yes Illness, disability, mental health, medical treatment Data on ethnicity or race Yes Ethnic identity, migration, discrimination Trade union activities Sometimes Labor organizing, workplace disputes Social assistance Sometimes Welfare, disability benefits Administrative or criminal proceedings Sometimes Arrest, trial, legal dispute Genetic data Rare Hereditary conditions, genetic testing Biometric data Yes — by default Audio of a voice, video of a face

Audio and video recordings

An audio or video recording of an identifiable person is, in itself, personal data. When the narrator discusses any sensitive category above, you are processing sensitive personal data. This is the default in most oral history projects. Plan accordingly from the start.

Third-party data: Narrators frequently mention other people by name. When a narrator describes a colleague's political views or a family member's health condition, you are processing sensitive personal data about third parties who have not consented. See Section 2.7 for strategies.

docs.title: 2.4 Informed Consent
docs.text: Informed consent is the cornerstone of ethical oral history practice and a legal requirement under Swiss law.

docs.title: What consent must cover
docs.text: Valid consent must be informed (narrator understands what, why, how, who, where, how long), voluntary (no pressure or coercion), specific (covers stated purposes), documented (written — UZH standard), and explicit for sensitive data (implied consent is insufficient).

docs.title: The timing tension
docs.text:
There is a tension between oral history practice and data protection law:

  • OHA best practice recommends signing the legal release after the interview, so the narrator can decide about sensitive topics that emerged.
  • Swiss data protection law requires information before or at the point of data collection.

docs.title: Recommended: Two-stage consent
docs.text:
Stage 1 — Before the interviewStage 2 — After the interview

Provide the narrator with a written information sheet describing:

  • The project, its objectives, and the lead researcher
  • Types of data collected (audio, video, transcript)
  • Processing purposes and methods
  • Storage location and security measures
  • Who will have access
  • Retention period (default: 10 years per UZH standard)
  • Narrator's rights (access, correction, deletion, withdrawal)

Obtain preliminary consent to record the interview.

Review the consent form together with the narrator. The narrator now makes informed decisions about:

  • Access restrictions and sensitivity tier
  • Pseudonymization or name attribution
  • Embargo periods
  • Exclusion of specific passages
  • Conditions for third-party anonymization
  • Consent to secondary use / future research

Obtain the final, detailed consent (the legal release).

This two-stage process satisfies both the OHA principle of narrator agency and the legal requirement for prior information.

docs.title: Withdrawal
docs.text: Under Swiss law, a narrator may withdraw consent at any time for future processing. Per the UZH consent form: data collected up to that point must be deleted and destroyed, unless doing so would make it impossible to achieve the research objectives or seriously jeopardize their achievement. The consent form should clearly explain what happens upon withdrawal.

docs.title: Secondary use
docs.text:
If archived oral histories may be reused, the original consent must address this. Options:

  • Project-specific: Limited to current project only — reuse requires re-consent
  • Broad consent: Future academic research within a defined scope
  • Tiered consent (recommended): The narrator chooses from options

docs.title: When is ethical review required?
docs.text:
If the HRA applies → Mandatory approval by KEK before data collection.

If the HRA does not apply (most oral history) → No legal requirement, but UZH's Ethics Policy recommends review for projects involving:

  • Vulnerable groups
  • Sensitive data categories
  • Potential psychological or social harm
  • Deception or incomplete disclosure
  • Dependency relationships

docs.title: Self-assessment checklist
docs.text:
• Does your project involve vulnerable groups (minors, persons with mental illness/dementia, persons in prison, persons incapable of judgment)?
  • Will you collect sensitive data (religion, politics, health, ethnicity, criminal proceedings, sexual orientation)?
  • Could participation psychologically harm the narrator (re-traumatization, emotional distress)?
  • Could participation socially harm the narrator (reputational damage)?
  • Is there a dependency relationship between you and the narrator?
  • Does your funder or publisher require evidence of ethical review?

If you answered "yes" to any → contact your faculty's ethics committee.

UZH Ethics Commission

The university-wide UZH Ethics Commission (Center for Ethics) supports ethical awareness but is not responsible for evaluating individual research projects. For project-level review, contact your faculty's ethics committee. → ethik.uzh.ch

docs.title: Personality rights (Art. 28 Swiss Civil Code)
docs.text:
Every person has the right to protection against unlawful interference with their personality:

  • Right to one's own image and voice — publishing a recording without consent violates this
  • Right to privacy — disclosing private facts without consent is unlawful
  • Right to honor — content damaging someone's reputation may be unlawful

Consent from the narrator addresses these for the narrator's personality. Third parties remain protected.

docs.title: Copyright in interviews
docs.text: Copyright is shared: the narrator holds copyright in their spoken words (if meeting the originality threshold), the interviewer may hold copyright in the questions and structure, and the recording is a neighboring right held by the producer. The consent/release form should include a transfer or license of rights permitting the intended use.

docs.title: 2.7 Third-Party Rights
docs.text: When a narrator says "My supervisor was a terrible bully who had a drinking problem," you are recording identifiable personal data about someone who has not consented.

docs.title: Practical strategies by tier
docs.text:
Tier 1–2 (Open)Tier 3 (Restricted)Tier 4–5 (Closed)
  • Anonymize all third parties by default unless they are public figures in their public capacity
  • Use descriptions: "a colleague" instead of "Dr. Müller"
  • Obtain consent from named third parties if their identifiable data is central and will be published
  • Anonymize third parties in any publicly accessible output
  • Original recording with identifiable third-party data retained under restricted access
  • Note in metadata which passages contain third-party data
  • Third-party data may be retained in the original recording
  • Access restrictions protect third-party interests during embargo
  • Plan for anonymization or review when access opens

docs.title: 2.8 Third-Party Data Processing at UZH
docs.text:
When you engage external service providers (transcription, cloud storage, hosting), UZH's standardized framework applies:

For ICT services (cloud transcription, hosted platforms):

  1. GTC of the Swiss Conference on Informatics (CSI) for ICT Services (Jan 2015)
  2. GTC DP Outsourcing IT UZH (July 2018)
  3. UZH Confidentiality Declaration (signed by all persons with access to UZH data)

For non-ICT services (human transcribers, research assistants):

  1. GTC DP Processing Third Parties UZH (July 2018)
  2. UZH Confidentiality Declaration

For processing in countries without adequate data protection:

  • Add: EU Standard Contractual Clauses (C(2010)593) adapted for UZH

Consumer AI tools

Free consumer-grade transcription tools (free Otter.ai, YouTube auto-caption, consumer Google Docs voice typing) typically do not offer data processing agreements and may use your data for model training. Do not use these for oral history recordings at any sensitivity tier.

docs.title: 2.9 Data Sharing and Cross-Border Transfer
docs.text:
Within UZH: Generally permissible if recipients need the data for a stated purpose, access is limited by tier, and sharing is consistent with the narrator's consent.

With external partners: Requires a Data Transfer and Use Agreement (DTUA) or the UZH Master Data Sharing Agreement (Controller–Controller).

Cross-border transfer: Verify that the destination country provides adequate protection (Federal Council list). If not → use EU/Swiss Standard Contractual Clauses or obtain explicit consent for the transfer.

docs.title: 2.10 Vulnerable Populations
docs.text:
Groups requiring additional protections:

  • Persons with mental illness or dementia
  • Persons in prison or acute emergencies
  • Minors (under 18)
  • Persons incapable of judgment
  • Communities with historically complex relationships with researchers
  • Persons in dependency relationships with the researcher
  • Persons whose testimony could endanger them (undocumented migrants, whistleblowers)

Key considerations: Ensure capacity to consent. Consider whether participation could cause harm. Offer pseudonyms from the outset. For specific communities, consider a community advisory board. Classify at Tier 3 or higher. Plan for interviewer wellbeing when working with traumatic testimony.

docs.title: 2.11 Record-Keeping Obligations
docs.text:
Processing activities register: Document your oral history project with purpose, data categories, recipients, retention periods, and security measures.

Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA): Required when processing poses high risk. Likely needed for large-scale sensitive oral history projects. Even when not legally required, a DPIA is good practice.

docs.title: 2.12 Legal Checklist
docs.text:
• Identified applicable law (IDG primary + nFADP + HRA if health-related)
  • Assessed sensitivity tier
  • Prepared informed consent forms (adapted from UZH template, tier-specific)
  • Determined whether ethical review is needed
  • Completed or planned a Data Management Plan
  • Assessed third-party processing needs and prepared UZH documents
  • Planned anonymization/pseudonymization strategy for third-party data
  • Verified cross-border transfer compliance if data leaves Switzerland
  • Considered a DPIA if processing sensitive data at scale
  • Registered processing activities
  • Consulted UZH Delegate for Data Protection for project-specific questions

docs.title: Key Contacts
docs.text: 📧 UZH Delegate for Data Protectiondatenschutz@dsd.uzh.ch · Stampfenbachstrasse 73, 8006 Zurich 🏛 Cantonal Data Protection Commissioner (Zurich)datenschutz.ch 🇨🇭 Federal Data Protection Commissioner (FDPIC/EDÖB)edoeb.admin.ch ⚕️ Cantonal Ethics Commission Zurich (KEK)zh.ch/ethikkommission 🎓 UZH Ethics Commission (Center for Ethics)ethik.uzh.ch

docs.title: Sensitivity Classification Model
docs.text:
Every oral history project should be assigned a sensitivity tier before data collection begins. The tier determines consent requirements, storage security, access restrictions, and which tools you can use. Assign the tier based on the most sensitive content you expect — you can always restrict more, but opening access later requires re-consent.

How to use this model: Read through the five tiers below. Use the interactive assessment tool to determine your project's tier based on specific questions about your content, narrators, and context. The tier you assign will carry through to consent forms, storage decisions, tool choices, and archiving conditions.

docs.title: The Five Tiers
docs.text:
1 Open

No restrictions on access. Full public availability. Name attribution.

Content: Non-sensitive. Public figures discussing their published work, professional methodology, or matters already in the public record. Identification: Narrator named with full attribution. Consent: Standard informed consent. Single-stage may suffice. Storage: Standard UZH infrastructure. Full-disk encryption on devices. Access: Open access. No restrictions. CC license recommended. Tools: All tools suitable — including cloud-based transcription and storage. Third parties: Anonymize by default unless public figures in public capacity. Example: A retired professor discusses their published research and academic career for a university history project. 2 Open with Conditions

Publicly accessible with conditions: attribution required, specific excerpts may be excluded, no commercial use.

Content: Mildly sensitive. Narrator may share personal opinions or anecdotes they're comfortable with in a controlled context but want some boundaries. Identification: Named or pseudonymized — narrator chooses. Consent: Two-stage consent. Consent form specifies excluded passages and conditions. Storage: Standard UZH infrastructure. Full-disk encryption. Access: Public with conditions (attribution, no commercial, excerpts excluded). CC BY-NC-SA or similar. Tools: Cloud tools with DPA acceptable. Consumer tools still not recommended. Third parties: Anonymize all third parties by default. Example: A retired professional discusses workplace culture and industry trends. Some anecdotes about specific incidents are excluded from the public transcript at the narrator's request. 3 Restricted Access

Access restricted to registered researchers who apply and justify their need.

Content: Sensitive. Political opinions, workplace conflicts, personal views on controversial topics, ethnicity and identity, religion. Content that could cause reputational harm if widely disclosed. Identification: Pseudonymized recommended. Named only with strong justification and explicit consent. Consent: Two-stage consent with explicit consent for each sensitive category. Consent form specifies access conditions. Storage: UZH infrastructure with access controls. Encrypted containers for portable copies. No personal cloud storage. Access: Registered researchers only. Application required. Research purpose must be stated. Tools: Local transcription (Whisper) preferred. Cloud tools only with DPA and Swiss/EU data location. No US-based services. Third parties: All third parties anonymized in transcripts. Original recordings retained under restricted access. De-identification log maintained. Example: Interviews on workplace culture in a politically sensitive institution. Narrators discuss management practices, internal conflicts, and their personal political views. 4 Closed / Embargoed

Sealed for a defined period (10, 25, or 50 years). Access limited to the original research team during embargo.

Content: Highly sensitive. Trauma, health conditions, legal matters, substance use, intimate relationships. Content that could cause serious harm to the narrator or third parties if disclosed. Identification: Pseudonymized in all outputs. De-identification key stored separately under highest security. Consent: Two-stage consent with explicit consent for each sensitive category. Embargo period specified. Consent addresses what happens when embargo expires. Storage: UZH Research Storage or ScienceCloud only. VeraCrypt containers. No cloud services for original recordings. Physical security for consent forms. Access: Original research team only during embargo. Post-embargo: review, anonymize, and potentially reclassify to Tier 3 or 2. Tools: Local transcription only (Whisper or manual). No cloud processing. Swiss-hosted encrypted communication only. Third parties: Retained in original recording. Access restrictions protect third-party interests. Anonymization plan for post-embargo. Example: Oral histories of individuals discussing their experiences with illness, domestic abuse, or legal proceedings. A 25-year embargo protects all parties. 5 Highly Restricted

Access limited to specifically named individuals. Possible destruction after research concludes.

Content: Extremely sensitive. Ongoing legal matters, whistleblower testimony, content that could endanger the narrator's safety, security, or liberty. Active threats. Identification: Pseudonymized throughout. Consider whether even the pseudonymized version should be retained. Consent: Two-stage consent with maximum specificity. Consent form names exactly who may access. Addresses conditions for destruction. Re-consent required for any change in access. Storage: UZH Research Storage with named-individual access only. VeraCrypt containers with separate passwords. Air-gapped backup if appropriate. Physical security. Access: Named individuals only. No application-based access. Possible scheduled destruction. Tools: Local transcription only. Manual preferred over AI. Air-gapped devices for highest risk. Swiss encrypted communication only (ProtonMail, Threema). Third parties: Maximum protection. Consider whether third-party data should be recorded at all. Example: A whistleblower describes ongoing institutional misconduct. Disclosure could result in legal retaliation or personal danger. Access is limited to the lead researcher and one designated successor.

docs.title: Comparison Table
docs.text: Aspect Tier 1 Tier 2 Tier 3 Tier 4 Tier 5 Access Public Public + conditions Registered researchers Research team only Named individuals Identification Named Named or pseudonym Pseudonym recommended Pseudonym required Pseudonym required Consent type Standard Two-stage Two-stage + explicit Two-stage + explicit + embargo Maximum specificity Ethics review Not required Not required Recommended Recommended Strongly recommended Transcription Any tool Cloud with DPA Local preferred Local only Local/manual only Storage UZH standard UZH standard UZH + encryption UZH only + VeraCrypt UZH only + air-gap option Cloud services Permitted With DPA Swiss/EU only with DPA Not recommended Not permitted Third-party data Anonymize for publication Anonymize by default Anonymize in all outputs Protected by embargo Consider not recording Archive access Open CC with conditions Application-based Sealed (10/25/50 yr) Named individuals / destroy DPIA needed? No No Consider Yes (if large-scale) Yes

docs.title: Tier Assignment Principles
docs.text:
Assign based on the most sensitive content. If even one portion of an interview reaches Tier 4 sensitivity, the entire recording should be managed at Tier 4 — unless you separate the sensitive passages into a distinct, separately managed file.

Tiers can differ within a project. If you interview 20 narrators, some may be Tier 2 while others are Tier 4. Assign per-narrator, not per-project.

Narrators can influence the tier. The two-stage consent process lets the narrator choose a higher restriction than your default assessment. A narrator who you assessed as Tier 2 may request Tier 3 protections after the interview — always respect this.

Tiers can decrease over time (with re-consent). A Tier 4 interview may be reclassified to Tier 3 after the embargo expires, but only with the narrator's consent or after the conditions specified in the original consent are met.

When in doubt, tier up. It is easier and legally safer to restrict now and open later than to close access on material that has already been disclosed.

Use the assessment tool

Not sure which tier applies to your project? The interactive assessment walks you through questions about your content, narrators, and context, and recommends a tier with a tailored report.

docs.title: Glossary & References
docs.text: Key terms used throughout this guide, followed by primary references, legal sources, and further reading. Terms are defined in the context of Swiss oral history research at UZH.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Amtsgeheimnis (Official Secrecy) The duty of confidentiality that applies to all employees of cantonal institutions in Zurich, including UZH. Governed by the IDG. UZH employees do not need to sign a separate confidentiality declaration — they are bound by official secrecy by virtue of their employment. Anonymization The irreversible removal of all identifying information from personal data, such that the data can no longer be linked to any individual. Truly anonymized data falls outside the scope of data protection law. Compare with: Pseudonymization. Biometric data Data resulting from specific technical processing of physical, physiological, or behavioral characteristics that allows or confirms unique identification of a natural person. In oral history, audio recordings (voice) and video recordings (face) constitute biometric data by default. Consent (informed) A freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous expression of will by which the data subject agrees to the processing of their personal data. For sensitive personal data under Swiss law, consent must be explicit (i.e., cannot be implied from behavior). See Consent Form Templates. Controller (Verantwortlicher) The person or body that determines the purposes and means of data processing. In a UZH oral history project, the lead researcher (or UZH as an institution) is typically the controller. Cross-border transfer The transfer of personal data to a country outside Switzerland. Permitted if the destination country provides adequate protection (Federal Council adequacy list), or if appropriate safeguards are in place (Standard Contractual Clauses, explicit consent). DaSCH Data and Service Center for the Humanities. A Swiss national infrastructure for long-term preservation and access of digital humanities research data. SNSF-supported. De-identification key A document that maps pseudonyms to real identifiers (e.g., "[NARRATOR-A]" → "Dr. Maria Müller"). Must be stored separately from the anonymized data, at the same or higher security level as the original data. DMP (Data Management Plan) A document that describes how research data will be collected, processed, stored, shared, and archived. Required by SNSF (via mySNF) and ERC. See Project Planning. DPA (Data Processing Agreement) A contract between a controller and a processor that specifies data processing conditions, security measures, breach notification, and sub-processing restrictions. Required when using external services (transcription, cloud storage). DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment / DSFA) A systematic analysis of a data processing operation to assess its risks to data subjects and identify mitigation measures. Required for high-risk processing under both the nFADP and GDPR. Recommended for large-scale oral history projects with sensitive data. DSG / nFADP The Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (Datenschutzgesetz), revised version in force since 1 September 2023. Also referred to as nFADP (new Federal Act on Data Protection) or revDSG. Applies to private persons and federal bodies. See Legal & Ethical Framework. Dublin Core A set of 15 metadata elements (title, creator, subject, description, date, etc.) widely used for resource description. The SNSF requires Dublin Core as a minimum metadata standard. See Processing & Documentation. EDÖB / FDPIC The Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (Eidgenössischer Datenschutz- und Öffentlichkeitsbeauftragter). The Swiss federal supervisory authority for data protection. → edoeb.admin.ch Embargo A restriction on access to archived materials for a specified period (commonly 10, 25, or 50 years from recording). Used for Tier 4 oral histories. After the embargo expires, access may be opened (often to Tier 3 conditions) if the consent form permits. FAIR principles Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable. A set of principles for research data management endorsed by SNSF, ERC, and the broader scientific community. "As open as possible, as closed as necessary." GTC (General Terms and Conditions) Standard contract terms. UZH has specific GTC documents for third-party data processing: GTC CSI (ICT services), GTC DP Outsourcing IT (IT services), GTC DP Processing Third Parties (non-IT services). See Storage & Security. HRA / HFG (Human Research Act) The Swiss Humanforschungsgesetz. Governs research on human diseases and the structure/function of the human body. Requires approval by a cantonal ethics commission (KEK). Most oral history does not fall under the HRA. IDG The Zurich Act on Information and Data Protection (Gesetz über die Information und den Datenschutz). The primary data protection law for UZH as a cantonal institution. Governs all data processing by UZH employees. KEK Cantonal Ethics Commission Zurich (Kantonale Ethikkommission Zürich). Approves research projects falling under the HRA. → zh.ch/ethikkommission Narrator The person being interviewed in an oral history project. Also referred to as "interviewee," "informant," or "participant" in other disciplines. This guide uses "narrator" following OHA convention, emphasizing their active role in shaping the testimony. OHA Oral History Association. The professional organization for oral historians, primarily US-based but internationally influential. Its Principles and Best Practices (revised 2018) are a key reference. → oralhistory.org Personality rights (Art. 28 ZGB) Swiss civil law protections for personal attributes: image, voice, privacy, and honor. Publishing a recording of someone without their consent may violate these rights. Consent from the narrator addresses this for the narrator; third parties remain protected. Processor (Auftragsbearbeiter) A person or body that processes personal data on behalf of the controller. In oral history: transcription services, cloud hosting providers, external research assistants. Requires a DPA and UZH GTC. Pseudonymization Replacing identifying information with codes or pseudonyms while maintaining a key that could re-link the data to the individual. Pseudonymized data is still personal data under Swiss law. Compare with: Anonymization. Sensitive personal data Under Swiss law (nFADP Art. 5(c), IDG): data on religious, philosophical, political, or trade union views/activities; health data; data on ethnicity; genetic data; biometric data; social assistance data; data on administrative or criminal proceedings or sanctions. Processing requires explicit consent. Sensitivity tier The five-level classification model used in this guide to determine security, access, and consent requirements: Tier 1 (Open), Tier 2 (Conditional), Tier 3 (Restricted), Tier 4 (Embargoed), Tier 5 (Highly Restricted). See Sensitivity Classification Model. SWISSUbase A national platform for sharing and preserving social science and humanities research data in Switzerland. FAIR-compliant. Supports multiple access levels. SNSF-recommended. → swissubase.ch Two-stage consent The consent process recommended in this guide: Stage 1 (Information Sheet before the interview) + Stage 2 (Consent Form after the interview). Satisfies both OHA narrator-agency principles and Swiss data protection information obligations. VeraCrypt Free, open-source encryption software for creating encrypted volumes (containers) and encrypting entire drives. Used in this guide for file-level encryption of oral history data, especially for Tiers 3–5. Whisper An open-source AI speech recognition model developed by OpenAI. When run locally (not via API), recordings never leave the researcher's device, making it suitable for all sensitivity tiers. See Transcription Tools.

docs.title: Anonymization Tools
docs.text:
All anonymization tools run locally and are suitable for every tier. The tier relevance here is about when anonymization is required, not tool suitability.

Tool Type Price Tiers When to use Manual redaction in transcript Text editing Free 1–5 Always applicable. Search-and-replace names with [PSEUDONYM-A], [PSEUDONYM-B]. Required from Tier 2+ for third-party data; from Tier 3+ for narrator data in publications. OpenAIRE Amnesia Tabular data anonymization Free (open source) 1–5 Structured/coded data only (not transcripts). Useful if exporting quantitative summaries. Audacity Audio editing Free (open source) 1–5 Silence or tone-replace identifying passages in audio. Required from Tier 3+ before sharing audio. DaVinci Resolve Video editing Free version available 1–5 Blur faces, silence audio passages, remove location identifiers from video. Required from Tier 3+ before sharing video. OBS Studio + filters Video processing Free (open source) 1–5 Apply blur filters in real-time or post-processing. Useful during remote interviews for on-the-fly anonymization. ATLAS.ti / NVivo / MAXQDA QDA software University licenses 1–5 Code and manage anonymized passages within the analysis workflow. Built-in annotation supports de-identification tracking.

De-identification log: Maintain a separate, securely stored log of all replacements made (e.g., "Dr. Müller → [COLLEAGUE-A]"). This log should be stored separately from the anonymized data and protected at the same or higher security level as the original data.

docs.title: Audio Recording Equipment
docs.text:
All audio recorders and microphones listed here are local, offline hardware — recordings are stored on an SD card or connected device. No data leaves your possession, so all are suitable for every sensitivity tier.

Equipment Type Approx. price Tiers Notes Zoom H6essential Portable recorder (6 inputs) CHF 350–400 1–5 Current model replacing H6. 4 XLR inputs, backup recording, 32-bit float. Zoom H1essential Compact portable recorder CHF 100–120 1–5 Budget-friendly, 32-bit float, good built-in mics. Simple one-on-one interviews. Tascam DR-40X Portable recorder (4 inputs) CHF 200–250 1–5 Reliable workhorse. Dual recording mode (backup at lower level). XLR inputs. Tascam DR-07X Compact portable recorder CHF 100–130 1–5 Good budget option. Adjustable stereo mics. USB audio interface mode. Rode NT1 5th Gen Large-diaphragm condenser mic CHF 250–280 1–5 Studio-quality for stationary setups. Very low noise. USB and XLR. Rode PodMic USB Dynamic USB/XLR mic CHF 110–130 1–5 Good for voice, rejects background noise. Plug-and-play USB option. Audio-Technica AT2020 Condenser mic CHF 100–120 1–5 Affordable studio-quality. Good for quiet indoor settings. Rode Lavalier II Clip-on lavalier mic CHF 100–120 1–5 Discrete body mic. Good for video interviews where the mic shouldn't be visible. Rode Wireless GO II Wireless lavalier system (2 transmitters) CHF 280–320 1–5 Two wireless mics → excellent for interviewer + narrator. Built-in recording as backup.

Recommended minimum setup: Zoom H1essential or Tascam DR-07X + a quiet room. For higher quality: Zoom H6essential + 2× Rode PodMic or lavalier mics.

File formats: Always record in WAV (uncompressed) at 48 kHz / 24-bit minimum. 32-bit float recorders (Zoom essential series) eliminate the risk of clipping.

docs.title: Encryption & Security Tools
docs.text:
Tool Type Price Tiers Notes VeraCrypt Full-disk / container encryption Free (open source) 1–5 Create encrypted containers for oral history files. Cross-platform. Essential for USB drives and portable storage. BitLocker Full-disk encryption (Windows) Included in Windows Pro/Enterprise 1–5 Encrypt entire drives. Ensure it's enabled on any laptop with oral history data. Required for all tiers. FileVault Full-disk encryption (macOS) Included in macOS 1–5 Encrypt entire drives. Enable in System Preferences → Security & Privacy. Required for all tiers. 7-Zip File-level encryption Free (open source) 1–5 AES-256 encrypted archives. Good for sharing individual files via secure transfer. Cryptomator Cloud encryption overlay Free (open source) 1–5 Encrypts files before they go to cloud storage (OneDrive, Dropbox, etc.). Elevates cloud storage security — enables Tier 3–4 data on cloud services. KeePass / KeePassXC Password manager (local) Free (open source) 1–5 Local database, no cloud. Preferred for Tiers 4–5 where cloud-synced passwords are undesirable. Bitwarden Password manager (cloud) Free tier / $10/yr premium 1–3 Cloud-synced. Open source. EU/US servers. Good for team password sharing. For Tiers 4–5: prefer KeePass (local).

Minimum requirements for all tiers: - Full-disk encryption on all devices (BitLocker/FileVault) - Encrypted USB drives for transport (VeraCrypt) - Strong, unique passwords for all project accounts (password manager)

Additional for Tiers 4–5: - File-level encryption (VeraCrypt containers or Cryptomator) - Restricted access logging - Physical security measures (locked cabinet for consent forms, restricted office access)

docs.title: Remote / Online Recording Tools
docs.text:
Tool Type Price Tiers Notes Zoom (video conferencing) Video call with local recording UZH license (free for staff) 1–3 Use "local recording" (not cloud). Record separate audio tracks if possible. For Tiers 4–5: use only as communication channel, record locally via OBS. Riverside.fm Remote recording platform Free tier (limited) / ~$15/mo 1–3 Records locally on each participant's device → higher quality than Zoom. Separate audio + video tracks. Cloud upload involved — requires DPA. Zencastr Remote audio recording Free tier / ~$20/mo 1–3 Local recording, separate tracks. Audio-focused. Cloud component — requires DPA. OBS Studio Screen/video recording (local) Free (open source) 1–5 Fully local recording of your own screen/camera. Can capture Zoom calls as backup. No data leaves device. Cleanfeed Remote audio studio Free tier / £22/mo 1–2 Professional remote audio recording. Low latency. No video. UK-based — check DPA.

Recommended for UZH: Zoom (institutional license) with local recording enabled as baseline. For higher quality: Riverside.fm or Zencastr for the actual recording, with Zoom as the communication channel.

Remote recording and data protection

Cloud-based recording tools (Riverside, Zencastr) process personal data on external servers. Verify the service's data processing location, review their DPA, and apply UZH third-party processing requirements (see §2.8). For Tiers 4–5, consider local-only recording: each participant records locally using OBS or a phone recorder, then transfers files via secure channel.

docs.title: Secure Communication Tools
docs.text: Tool Type Tiers Notes UZH institutional email Email 1–3 Standard for official communication. Encryption available via S/MIME certificates. Not end-to-end encrypted by default — avoid for Tier 4–5 content in message body. UZH institutional email + S/MIME Encrypted email 1–5 With S/MIME certificate enabled, suitable for all tiers. Recipient must also support S/MIME. ProtonMail Encrypted email 1–5 Swiss-based (Geneva). End-to-end encryption. Free tier available. Good for narrators who prefer privacy. Threema Encrypted messaging 1–5 Swiss-based (Pfäffikon SZ). End-to-end encryption. No phone number required. ~CHF 5 one-time. Signal Encrypted messaging 1–4 End-to-end encryption. Free. Open source. Servers in the US but messages are encrypted. Avoid for Tier 5 where Swiss-only infrastructure is preferred. SWITCHfilesender Large file transfer 1–4 Available to Swiss universities. Up to 250 GB. Files encrypted in transit. Temporary links. Not designed for highly restricted long-term access. UZH OneDrive / SharePoint File sharing 1–3 UZH-provided Microsoft 365. Swiss/EU data centers (verify current policy). For Tiers 4–5: use UZH Research Storage instead.

docs.title: Swiss University Storage Solutions
docs.text:
Service Provider Tiers Notes UZH Research Storage UZH Central IT 1–5 Primary storage for active research data. Backed up. Access controlled. Recommended for all tiers. UZH ScienceCloud UZH S3IT 1–5 Cloud computing and storage for research. Swiss-hosted. Suitable for processing sensitive data including Tier 5. UZH OneDrive / SharePoint Microsoft 365 (UZH license) 1–3 Swiss/EU data centers. Good for collaboration. For Tiers 4–5: data residency and access logging may not meet requirements — use UZH Research Storage. SWITCHdrive SWITCH (Swiss university network) 1–3 Swiss-hosted cloud storage. 50 GB free for Swiss university members. Good for sharing with collaborators at other Swiss universities. Not designed for highly restricted access. SWITCHfilesender SWITCH 1–4 Temporary large file transfer (up to 250 GB). Swiss-hosted. Temporary links — not for long-term storage. SWITCHengines SWITCH 1–4 OpenStack cloud infrastructure. Swiss data centers. For compute and storage. Requires technical setup.

Recommendation: Use UZH Research Storage for primary data. SWITCHdrive for collaboration. VeraCrypt-encrypted containers for portable copies.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Recommended equipment, software, and services for oral history research at UZH. Each tool is rated by sensitivity tier suitability to help you make data-protection-compliant choices.

Tier key: 1 Open 2 Conditional 3 Restricted 4 Closed 5 Highly restricted 🎙 Audio Recording Equipment

Portable recorders, microphones, and lavalier systems for in-person interviews. All local hardware — suitable for every tier.

View equipment → 9 items 🎥 Video Recording Equipment

Cameras, tripods, and lighting for video interviews. Local hardware — tier concern is archiving and access, not recording.

View equipment → 7 items 🌐 Remote / Online Recording

Zoom, Riverside, OBS, and other tools for remote interviews. Cloud-based tools require DPAs; local tools (OBS) work for all tiers.

View tools → 5 items ✍️ Transcription Tools

AI-powered and manual transcription options. Local tools (Whisper) suit all tiers; cloud services are restricted to Tiers 1–3 with DPA.

View tools → 9 items 🔒 Secure Communication

Encrypted email, messaging, and file transfer for contacting narrators and sharing materials. Swiss-hosted options for highest tiers.

View tools → 7 items 🛡 Encryption & Security

Disk encryption, file-level encryption, and password management. Full-disk encryption is mandatory for all tiers; container encryption for Tiers 4–5.

View tools → 7 items 👤 Anonymization Tools

Text redaction, audio silencing, and video blurring. All local tools — the tier determines when anonymization is required, not which tool to use.

View tools → 6 items ☁️ Storage Solutions

UZH Research Storage, ScienceCloud, SWITCHdrive, and other Swiss university infrastructure. UZH-hosted options are recommended for Tiers 4–5.

View options → 6 items Last reviewed: February 2026. Prices are approximate CHF equivalents. Tool recommendations should be verified against current institutional licensing and data protection policies. This page is updated at least annually — check individual tool pages for detailed notes.

docs.text:

Recommended approach: - Tiers 1–3: Use local Whisper for first pass, then manually correct. Or use Amberscript with DPA. - Tiers 4–5: Use local Whisper or oTranscribe/f4transkript only. No cloud processing.

docs.title: Video Recording Equipment
docs.text:
Like audio hardware, cameras and accessories are local equipment — recordings stay on the SD card or device. Suitable for all tiers. The tier concern with video is primarily about archiving and access, not the recording itself.

Equipment Type Approx. price Tiers Notes Sony ZV-E10 II Mirrorless camera (APS-C) CHF 900–1,000 1–5 Excellent autofocus, flip screen, mic input, clean HDMI out. Good for talking-head setups. Sony ZV-1 II Compact vlog camera CHF 700–800 1–5 All-in-one solution, good built-in mic, but external audio recommended for archival quality. Canon EOS R50 Mirrorless camera CHF 750–850 1–5 Lightweight, good autofocus, 4K video. Popular entry-level option. Logitech Brio 500/505 Webcam (USB) CHF 120–150 1–3 Budget option for remote or casual recordings. Good 1080p, light correction. Not archival quality — avoid for Tiers 4–5 where preservation matters. Manfrotto Compact Action Tripod CHF 60–80 1–5 Budget-friendly, stable for talking-head video. Elgato Key Light Mini LED panel light CHF 80–100 1–5 Soft, adjustable lighting. USB-C powered. Essential for consistent video quality. Neewer 2-pack LED panels Budget LED lights CHF 60–80 1–5 Two-light setup for interviews. Adjustable color temperature.

Recommended minimum setup: Sony ZV-E10 II or equivalent + tripod + one LED light + external audio recorder or lavalier mic (do not rely on camera audio alone for archival purposes).
